title: Optimal Ancestor Queries in an Unbalanced Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Input will consist of n, the size of the input array. This will be followed by n space-separated integers representing the pre-order traversal of a binary tree.
statement.outputFormat: For each query, output the minimum depth of the common ancestor of i and j on a new line.

examples.explanation: For the first query, the common ancestor of 0 and 1 is node 2. The minimum depth of node 2 is 2. For the second query, the common ancestor of 0 and 3 is node 2. The minimum depth of node 2 is 2. For the third query, the common ancestor of 2 and 4 is node 4. The minimum depth of node 4 is 1. For the fourth query, the common ancestor of 3 and 4 is node 4. The minimum depth of node 4 is 1.

For the first query, the common ancestor of 0 and 1 is node 2. The minimum depth of node 2 is 2. For the second query, the common ancestor of 1 and 4 is node 2. The minimum depth of node 2 is 2. For the third query, the common ancestor of 2 and 5 is node 5. The minimum depth of node 5 is 1. For the fourth query, the common ancestor of 3 and 5 is node 5. The minimum depth of node 5 is 1. For the fifth query, the common ancestor of 2 and 6 is node 3. The minimum depth of node 3 is 2.

hints.text: You can solve this problem using a combination of DFS and Segment Trees.

editorial.title: Solution Overview
editorial.intuition: Use a Segment Tree to store the minimum depth of each node. When performing a DFS traversal, update the Segment Tree at each node.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a Segment Tree to store the minimum depth of each node. When performing a DFS traversal, update the Segment Tree at each node. Finally, use the Segment Tree to query the minimum depth of the common ancestor of each pair of nodes.
editorial.dryRun: Let's say we have a binary tree with nodes at positions [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]. We can represent the tree as a 0-indexed array, where each element is a node's position. We can create a Segment Tree of size 5, with each node storing the minimum depth of its corresponding leaf node. During the DFS traversal, we update the Segment Tree at each node.
commonMistakes: Not updating the Segment Tree correctly
Using a Segment Tree of the wrong size
